title: What to Do When Fear Arises During a Psychedelic Journey
body:
What to Do When Fear Arises During a Psychedelic Journey

1. When the Shadow Creeps In: My First Encounter with Fear on Mushrooms

Fear. It can sneak up in the most unexpected moments, especially when you’ve set out on a psychedelic journey that promised insights, healing, or just pure wonder. I remember my first significant encounter with fear during a psilocybin trip vividly. The day had been calm, filled with the usual pre-journey rituals - setting intentions, tuning into my breath, checking the environment. Everything seemed under control until, somewhere around hour two, the colors deepened, the patterns undulated, and suddenly, a wave of panic surged like a rogue tide.

Not fear in the sense of a jump-scare or a simple anxious thought. No, this was a primal, gut-level terror that gripped me with the force of a tidal wave. My heart pounded, my chest tightened, and my mind spun stories darker than the night outside. I was caught in a loop of "what ifs," facing versions of myself I hadn’t dared meet before. That moment felt like a window into a wilderness of the self, dense with ancient fears and unresolved trauma.

What I didn’t know then, and what took me years to fully grasp, was that fear during a psychedelic experience is not an enemy but part of the medicine itself. It’s a gatekeeper to deeper healing, a messenger from buried parts of the psyche demanding attention. This fear is often misunderstood or feared itself. Many retreat from it or try to suppress it, but doing so can stifle the growth these journeys hold. Like a wild animal glimpsed in the dark, fear invites respect and curiosity, not resistance.

Over time, I've learned that fear during these sessions isn’t just some random hiccup; it’s a signal from parts of the brain and psyche wrestling for voice or release. And if you can meet it with the right mindset, you might find it transforming into deep insight and relief. My experiences echo those of countless others reported in the psychedelic community - fear is a universal visitor, but how we respond changes everything.

So, what are you supposed to do when fear visits? Should you fight it? Flee? Freeze? Or can you sit quietly and listen? This article is for anyone who’s been touched by that shadow on a psychedelic journey and wants to understand this visitor better. I’ll share what neuroscience says about fear in these states, dive into research from folks like Robin Carhart-Harris and Roland Griffiths, and offer practical ways to meet fear with courage and compassion. Because the truth is, fear doesn’t have to be the end of the road. Sometimes, it’s the beginning of the most meaningful part.

2. Understanding Fear During Psychedelic Experiences: Brain Chemistry and Beyond

Fear is an ancient biological response designed to keep us safe from harm. When you’re tripping on psychedelics like psilocybin or LSD, this response does not turn off - instead, it transforms. Psychedelics act primarily on serotonin receptors in the brain, especially the 5-HT2A receptor, which modulates perception, emotion, and cognition. But these chemicals don’t just dial up the happy feelings - they amplify everything, including fear.

Robin Carhart-Harris and his team at Imperial College London have mapped how psychedelics reduce activity in the brain’s default mode network (DMN), a system associated with rigid self-referential thoughts. This reduction creates a state called “ego dissolution,” where boundaries between self and environment blur. In this state, the brain’s fear circuits, especially the amygdala, become hypersensitive or, in some cases, disinhibited. What this means is that latent anxieties, repressed memories, or unresolved emotions can surface with startling vividness.

The amygdala, the brain’s fear center, is highly active during threatening situations. Under psychedelics, its activity can either increase or decrease depending on context and individual differences. This dynamic explains why some trips feel terrifying but others deeply calming. Psychedelics also enhance connectivity between brain regions that don’t usually communicate, allowing new emotional patterns to emerge. This rewiring can expose traumatic memories or fears wrapped in layers of avoidance and denial.

Psychologist Stanislav Grof, one of the early pioneers of psychedelic therapy, described this process as accessing the “perinatal matrices,” where birth trauma and primal fears reside. This perspective aligns with modern neuroscience showing that early traumatic experiences can imprint deep emotional scars, often hidden beneath daily awareness but accessible through altered states. When fear arises, it’s often tied to these deep subconscious layers.

Moreover, the physiological sensations linked with fear - racing heart, shortness of breath, muscle tension - are intensified under psychedelics due to heightened interoception, the brain’s awareness of bodily signals. This can create a feedback loop where physical fear symptoms feed emotional panic, escalating the experience. However, this same heightened body awareness can be a doorway to healing if met with calm observation.

In my own journeys and those I’ve witnessed, recognizing that fear is both a neurological and psychological event helps demystify it. Fear isn’t just a problem to be solved; it’s a message from the brain-body system trying to communicate something unresolved. This dual nature calls for compassion toward both mind and body, a lesson echoed by trauma expert Bessel van der Kolk in The Body Keeps the Score. Understanding the interplay between brain circuits and lived experience gives us the roadmap we need to work with fear rather than run from it.

3. What Research Tells Us About Fear and Psychedelic Therapy

Science has begun to catch up with what indigenous cultures and early psychonauts long understood - that psychedelic experiences are not always blissful rides but often involve moments of deep fear and discomfort that can lead to lasting change. Roland Griffiths, a foundational figure in psychedelic research at Johns Hopkins University, has documented the complex emotional landscapes participants traverse in clinical trials.

In one of Griffiths’ seminal studies, volunteers taking psilocybin reported intense fear and anxiety during sessions, yet many of these same individuals later rated the experience as one of the most meaningful and beneficial of their lives. This paradox highlights how fear within a psychedelic context can be a gateway to life-changing insight rather than a mere side effect to be avoided.

The “challenging experience” concept has been fleshed out in studies like those by Matthew Johnson and Bill Richards, who noted that about 30% to 40% of psilocybin sessions involve some degree of fear, panic, or paranoia. However, careful preparation, supportive settings, and integration work often turn these challenges into breakthroughs. Their research emphasized the importance of “set and setting” as buffers against overwhelming fear. A well-prepared mindset and a safe, reassuring environment help people tolerate and learn from the frightening parts.

Carhart-Harris’ team at Imperial used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to observe brain patterns during psychedelic trips. They found that while the amygdala can light up with fear-related signals, these activations are often transient and accompanied by increased global connectivity that allows cognitive reappraisal - a way for the brain to reinterpret scary stimuli. This neural flexibility is likely behind reports that fear experienced on psychedelics often leads to new perspectives on trauma or existential dread.

There’s also growing evidence from psychedelic-assisted therapy for PTSD, depression, and addiction that confronting fear under guided conditions can catalyze healing. For example, MAPS’ studies on MDMA-assisted psychotherapy, though not a classic psychedelic, show how controlled exposure to fear and trauma in a safe environment leads to symptom reduction. This work complements findings from psilocybin trials, reinforcing that fear is part of the healing journey.

For those who want to immerse themselves in these research narratives, Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind offers an accessible exploration of these studies, blending personal stories and scientific insights. Pollan captures the tension between fear and healing beautifully, showing how fear is woven into the fabric of change.

What emerges clearly from the research is that fear is not an obstacle so much as a signpost. It points to areas within the psyche that require attention and care. When approached with support and respect, what initially seems like terror can become a path to liberation.

4. Practical Steps to Face Fear During a Psychedelic Trip

When fear arrives mid-journey, it isn’t a call to panic or to quickly push it away. It’s a signal to engage with presence and curiosity. Over years of guiding myself and others, I’ve found a handful of practical actions that shift fear from a crashing wave into a flowing stream you can move through.

First, breathe. Sounds simple, but it’s the foundation. Fear typically triggers shallow, rapid breathing, which fuels the panic. Slow, intentional breaths activate the parasympathetic nervous system, calming the body. Try counting your breaths - inhale for a count of four, hold two, exhale for six. This shifts the nervous system towards relaxation. If you feel resistance, acknowledge it without judgment; the breath will steady in its own time.

Second, ground yourself by tuning into your body’s contact with the environment. Feel the texture of a blanket, the pressure of your feet on the floor, or the coolness of the air on your skin. Psychedelics heighten interoception, so using tactile sensations can anchor you to the present. I often recommend carrying a small object, like a smooth stone or a comforting piece of cloth, to hold during moments of fear.

Third, remind yourself that the experience is temporary. Fear during a trip can feel endless, but it almost always passes. Repeating a simple mantra like “This too shall pass” or “I am safe” can help create a mental buffer against spiraling panic.

Fourth, if possible, have a trusted sitter or guide nearby. Their calm presence can be a lifeline. Sometimes just hearing a reassuring voice or having someone hold your hand is enough to dissolve fear’s grip. If you’re alone, pre-journey journaling can prepare you with affirmations or intentions to call upon during challenging moments. A Journaling Notebook Leather Bound can be a perfect companion for this kind of preparation.

Another technique I find powerful is naming the fear out loud or in your mind. Identify if it’s panic, sadness, guilt, or a specific memory. Labeling emotions activates the prefrontal cortex, the brain’s thinking part, which helps regulate the amygdala’s alarm bells. This simple act of naming can pull you out of the chaos.

Finally, lean into compassion. Fear often arises from self-criticism or past trauma. Approach yourself as you would a scared child - with kindness and patience. I’ve seen this gentle stance shift entire experiences, turning moments of terror into openings for healing. Remember Bessel van der Kolk’s insights on how trauma lives in the body, and treat your nervous system tenderly.

These steps don’t promise instant eradication of fear, but they provide tools to sit with it rather than be overwhelmed. The key is not to fight or escape, but to meet fear with steady breath and soft eyes.

5. Common Challenges When Facing Fear and How to Work Through Them

Fear during psychedelics can feel like a riptide pulling you under, and it’s natural to want to react instinctively. But certain common pitfalls can make the situation worse. Recognizing these helps you better prepare and respond when fear visits.

One challenge is resistance. When fear emerges, the impulse is often to push it away, distract yourself, or escape. But psychedelics amplify avoidance as much as emotions. Fighting fear can escalate panic by creating internal conflict. Instead, I’ve learned through my own stumbles and from guiding others that leaning gently into fear - without judgment or analysis - often lets it dissipate faster. This is easier said than done, especially when the sensation is overwhelming.

Another challenge is isolation. Fear feels more intense when experienced alone. The psychedelic experience can make social connection tricky, especially if you’re in a private space or feeling vulnerable. This is why having a sitter, therapist, or trusted friend during sessions is recommended. If that’s not an option, pre-trip preparation can include phrases or mantras you plan to repeat or recorded voices you can listen to in moments of distress.

Physical discomfort can amplify fear, too. Sometimes, dehydration, hunger, or exhaustion worsen anxious feelings. Having water nearby, light snacks for post-peak, and comfortable surroundings help avoid this trap. I always prepare my space with pillows, blankets, and gentle lighting to reduce physical distractions that might feed fear.

One more common issue is unresolved trauma surfacing suddenly and uninvited. This can feel too much to bear without professional support. If this happens, it’s crucial not to judge yourself for the reactions or try to power through alone. Post-experience therapy or integration sessions with professionals trained in trauma-sensitive psychedelic work can be lifesaving. This is where books like The Body Keeps the Score provide invaluable guidance for understanding how trauma imprints on body and mind, helping you make sense of what emerges.

Lastly, impatience can be a stumbling block. Fear often arises in waves; people expect quick relief, but healing is nonlinear. Sitting with fear requires patience and allowing the journey to unfold in its own time. Sometimes the fear is a piece of a larger puzzle that only reveals itself slowly.

Working through fear means accepting these challenges as part of the process, not signs of failure. It’s about cultivating endurance, compassion, and trust - both in yourself and the medicine. Over time, each encounter with fear strengthens your capacity to hold discomfort without losing your center.

6. Integration and the Long-Term View: Fear as a Teacher in Daily Life

The moments of fear we experience during a psychedelic trip don’t end with the session. In fact, the real work often begins afterward, during integration. Integration is where you take the raw material of the journey - fear included - and fold it into your daily life in a way that fosters growth rather than repression.

One of the most powerful lessons I’ve learned is that fear during a trip often mirrors fears we carry in waking life. The psychedelic experience is like a microscope aimed at your inner world. When you examine those fears closely, you might discover they stem from old stories, patterns, or wounds you’ve long ignored. Integration offers the chance to rewrite those narratives and build resilience.

Keeping a journal is a practical tool here. Writing about your fears - what came up, how it felt, what it might be connected to - deepens understanding and creates mental distance. A beautiful leather-bound journal, like the Journaling Notebook I recommended earlier, invites this kind of reflection. When you revisit your notes weeks or months later, patterns emerge that can guide your healing journey.

Another aspect of integration involves body work. Since fear often resides physically, practices like yoga, breathwork, or somatic therapy help release the tension stored in muscles and nervous system. Bessel van der Kolk’s work has shown repeatedly that trauma - closely linked with fear - is held in the body, so mind-body methods complement mental processing.

Talking through your experience with trusted friends, therapists, or fellow travelers can be invaluable. Sharing fear can banish shame and reconnect you with the community dimension of healing. Many psychedelic therapy centers now emphasize integration for this reason.

Over the long term, the goal is to cultivate a different relationship with fear. Instead of avoidance, you build curiosity. Instead of reactivity, you find steadiness. This shift doesn’t happen overnight but grows through repeated encounters and reflection. Psychedelic fear becomes a teacher, revealing vulnerabilities but also your capacity to confront them.

When fear is integrated, it changes from a monster lurking in the shadows to a wise messenger guiding you toward wholeness. It can inspire new practices for self-care, boundaries, and emotional honesty that ripple out across your life far beyond the psychedelic session.

7. A Thought to Carry Forward: Fear as Part of the Journey, Not the Destination

Here’s a thought I keep coming back to: fear is not the enemy in psychedelic work. It’s a signpost, a signal, and sometimes the very doorway through which healing enters. We spend so much of our culture trying to eliminate fear, but psychedelic journeys remind us that fear is woven into life itself. It’s part of the human experience, especially when we get courageous enough to look inward.

In some ways, fear is a sign of progress. If you never feel afraid in a psychedelic session, you might be skimming the surface, staying safe but missing the medicine’s full offer. But when fear shows up, it means you’re touching something real and vulnerable - not just the shiny, Instagram-ready moments of bliss.

This doesn’t mean chasing fear or trying to manufacture it. Rather, it means learning to sit with it without fleeing. I think of fear as a fierce guardian standing at the gate of the unknown parts of your psyche. It’s protective, trying to keep you from pain, but if you learn to greet it with kindness and steady breath, it can become an ally rather than a block.

My hope is that anyone reading this will carry forward a new relationship to fear in their psychedelic explorations - one that embraces complexity over certainty, presence over avoidance. When you accept fear as part of the journey, not the destination, you open space for real change.

As Michael Pollan writes in How to Change Your Mind, these experiences can alter how we see ourselves and the world. Fear is part of that story. And if you can hold it gently, it might just lead you home.
